title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community film club. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Clara, The Box-Office Organiser
Clara began volunteering rather nervously when the old cinema reopened for monthly community screenings after years of darkness. She liked films, but her real task was making the entrance less chaotic. [47]On the first night, she discovered that the donated ticket printer worked only if someone held the paper tray at a particular angle.[/47] Rather than complain, she created a manual sign-in table and later persuaded a local shop to lend a simpler machine. [48]Her background in accounting helped her track ticket money, refunds and free seats without embarrassing people who could not pay.[/48] Clara says the work taught her that a welcoming queue matters almost as much as the film. She remembers one elderly couple arriving an hour early because they had not been to the cinema since it closed. By the third season, she had trained two teenagers to run the desk, which made the evenings feel less fragile.
texts.B:
### Ben, The Subtitles Volunteer
Ben joined the club in its second season after his neighbour, who was losing her hearing, said she avoided public screenings because subtitles were unreliable. [49]Her request was the direct reason he offered to help with accessible versions of the films.[/49] He expected the job to be a matter of typing dialogue, but it was more subtle than that. [50]He soon realised that captions also needed to show music, silence and background noises that changed the mood of a scene.[/50] At first, he worried viewers would notice every mistake. Instead, people thanked him for making them feel included without asking for special treatment. Ben quietly spent long evenings checking timing on a small laptop, sometimes rewinding the same joke fifteen times. He also persuaded the committee to mention subtitles on every poster. Now he argues that accessibility should be advertised clearly from the start, not added quietly at the end.
texts.C:
### Priya, The Young Filmmakers’ Mentor
Priya volunteered to run lively weekend workshops for teenagers who wanted to make short films about their neighbourhood. She had worked in advertising, so she knew how to plan shoots quickly, but she had to change her style almost immediately. [51]Her first mistake was giving the group a detailed camera manual, which they ignored until she replaced it with three simple demonstration tasks.[/51] After that practical change, the workshops became noisier but far more productive. Priya was impressed by how honestly the students filmed ordinary places, such as bus stops, corner shops, small laundrettes and empty playgrounds. [52]One boy’s short film about his mother’s night shifts made the audience unusually quiet and left his mother wiping her eyes.[/52] Priya says the project reminded her that young people do not need adults to speak for them. They simply need enough structure to make their own voices clear, confident and technically presentable.
texts.D:
### Mateo, The Outdoor Screening Technician
Mateo regularly helped with summer evening screenings in the park because his parents had watched films there when they first moved to the town. [53]That family link made him want the outdoor programme to survive.[/53] He was responsible for speakers, cables and the inflatable screen, which looked simple only when the weather behaved. [54]His hardest planning problem was timing each screening between sunset, park closing rules and complaints from nearby flats about late noise.[/54] A sudden wind once turned the screen into a sail, and he spent the next week adding extra safety ropes. [55]He also drew a seating plan that kept wheelchair spaces and pushchair routes clear without separating families from the main audience.[/55] Before the final screening, Mateo made everyone practise packing the equipment in the dark. [56]He later wrote a checklist for each cable and battery so the next technician would not have to rely on guesswork.[/56]

questions.question: mentions an unexpected practical fault with equipment used for admitting people?
questions.explanation: Clara discovered that the ticket printer only worked if the paper tray was held at an angle.

questions.question: says their financial experience helped them handle payments sensitively?
questions.explanation: Clara used her accounting background to track ticket money, refunds and free seats without embarrassment.

questions.question: says another person’s accessibility need directly led them to volunteer?
questions.explanation: Ben offered to help because his neighbour avoided screenings due to unreliable subtitles.

questions.question: learned that written support had to include sounds as well as spoken words?
questions.explanation: Ben realised captions needed to show music, silence and background noises.

questions.question: changed their teaching method after participants ignored detailed instructions?
questions.explanation: Priya replaced a detailed manual with three simple demonstration tasks.

questions.question: describes a film that caused an emotional reaction in a parent?
questions.explanation: A boy’s film about his mother’s night shifts left his mother wiping her eyes.

questions.question: was motivated by a family connection to the place where the activity happened?
questions.explanation: Mateo helped because his parents had watched films in the park.

questions.question: had to schedule activities around light, closing rules and complaints about noise?
questions.explanation: Mateo timed screenings between sunset, park rules and noise complaints.

questions.question: created a layout that allowed different audience members to stay with the group?
questions.explanation: Mateo drew a seating plan that kept wheelchair spaces and pushchair routes clear without separating families.

questions.question: made a written equipment guide for a future person in the same role?
questions.explanation: Mateo wrote a checklist for cables and batteries for the next technician.
